items.title: Scaling Basics: Vertical vs Horizontal
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Scaling Fundamentals

**Vertical scaling (scale up)**: bigger machine — more CPU/RAM/faster disks.
**Horizontal scaling (scale out)**: more machines sharing the load.

| | Vertical | Horizontal |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | none — same code | state, balancing, coordination |
| Ceiling | hardware limit (and price grows non-linearly) | practically unbounded |
| Availability | single machine = single point of failure | survives node loss |
| Databases | works great surprisingly long | hard (covered later: replication/sharding) |

## The honest engineering order

1. **Optimize first** — a SQL index or cache often beats new hardware for free (your SQL track!)
2. **Scale up** — trivially simple; a 64-core/512GB box handles more than most businesses ever need
3. **Scale out** — when you need availability, or the ceiling nears

The trap: jumping to distributed architecture for imaginary scale. A monolith on two load-balanced VMs with a solid SQL Server serves millions of requests/day. Complexity is a cost you pay forever; buy it only when the receipts justify it.

## What breaks when you go horizontal

The moment there are 2 app servers, these assumptions die:
- **In-memory session state** — user's next request hits the other server (next lesson)
- **Local file uploads** — saved to server A's disk, requested from server B
- **In-process cache coherence** — server A cached data server B just changed
- **Scheduled jobs** — now run twice!

Every one of these has a standard fix (external session/cache, object storage, distributed locks or single-runner election) — the rest of this track builds them out.

ASP.NET Core note: your apps are close-to-stateless by default (JWT auth, DI-scoped services) — the discipline is not breaking that property.
items.practiceTask: List every piece of state your current app process holds (session? IMemoryCache? static fields? local files? background timers?). For each, write what breaks with 2 instances behind a load balancer and the standard fix.

quiz.question: The recommended order of attack for a capacity problem is:
options: Always scale out — hardware is legacy
quiz.explanation: Optimization is free capacity; vertical scaling is complexity-free capacity; horizontal scaling buys unbounded capacity with permanent complexity.

items.title: Stateless Services and Session Management
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Statelessness: The Price of Admission to Scale-Out

A **stateless** service keeps nothing request-to-request in process memory. Any instance can serve any request → load balancing is trivial, instances are disposable (deploys, crashes, autoscaling are non-events).

## Where user state goes instead

| State | Stateless home |
|---|---|
| Authentication | **JWT / auth cookie** — the token carries identity; any server validates it |
| Session data (cart, wizard progress) | **Distributed cache** (Redis) or database, keyed by user/session id |
| Uploaded files | Object storage (Azure Blob/S3), never local disk |
| Server-side cache | Redis (shared) or per-instance cache *with acceptance of divergence* |

ASP.NET Core specifics:

```csharp
// Session backed by Redis instead of memory:
builder.Services.AddStackExchangeRedisCache(o => o.Configuration = "redis:6379");
builder.Services.AddSession();

// DataProtection keys MUST be shared across instances
// (else auth cookies from server A fail validation on server B!)
builder.Services.AddDataProtection()
    .PersistKeysToStackExchangeRedis(redis, "DataProtection-Keys");
```

That DataProtection detail is the classic 'works with one instance, random logouts with two' bug.

## Sticky sessions: the tempting trap

Load balancers offer **session affinity** (user pinned to one server). It 'fixes' statefulness but: kills even load distribution, deploys/scale-in log users out, one hot server melts while others idle. Acceptable as a *migration crutch*; wrong as architecture. (Exception: SignalR/WebSockets legitimately benefit from affinity — connection state is genuinely per-server; a backplane handles cross-server messaging.)

## Litmus test

"Can I kill any instance mid-day with zero user impact?" Yes = stateless enough. This same property is what makes containers/Kubernetes/serverless deployment models possible at all.
items.practiceTask: Run your app as two instances on different ports behind a simple round-robin (nginx or YARP). Log in, alternate requests between instances, and fix what breaks (session, DataProtection keys, any static state).

quiz.question: Two ASP.NET Core instances behind a load balancer: users get randomly logged out. Most likely cause:
options: DataProtection keys not shared — server B can't decrypt cookies issued by server A
quiz.explanation: Auth cookies are encrypted with DataProtection keys; unless persisted to shared storage, each instance generates its own.

items.title: Load Balancing: Algorithms and Health
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Load Balancers

The traffic distributor in front of your instances — and the component that turns 'a server died' from an outage into a non-event.

## Layer 4 vs Layer 7

- **L4 (transport)**: routes TCP connections by IP/port. Fast, dumb — can't see HTTP
- **L7 (application)**: reads HTTP — routes by path (`/api` vs `/static`), host, headers, cookies; can terminate TLS, compress, cache. Azure Application Gateway, nginx, YARP, HAProxy

Most app-level work happens at L7.

## Algorithms

| Algorithm | Behavior | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Round robin | rotate in order | uniform requests, default |
| **Least connections** | send to the least-busy | mixed request durations (usually best) |
| Weighted | capacity-proportional | heterogeneous instance sizes, canary (95/5) |
| IP hash | same client → same server | poor man's affinity |

## Health checks — the half people skip

The LB probes each instance (`GET /health` every ~10s); failures remove the instance from rotation until it recovers.

```csharp
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddSqlServer(connectionString)          // liveness of dependencies
    .AddCheck<QueueBacklogHealthCheck>("queue");
app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new() { Predicate = _ => false });  // process up?
app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready");                                    // deps up too?
```

**Liveness vs readiness** distinction matters: liveness = 'restart me if failing'; readiness = 'don't send traffic yet' (warming up, migrating, dependency down). Kubernetes and most orchestrators treat them differently.

Subtle trap: a health check that queries the DB, when the DB blips, marks **all** instances unhealthy simultaneously → LB has nobody to route to → total outage from a partial failure. Readiness may include dependencies; be careful what liveness includes.

Single LB = new single point of failure → real deployments run redundant LB pairs / cloud LBs are inherently redundant.
items.practiceTask: Put YARP or nginx in front of two instances of your API. Configure health checks, then kill one instance mid-traffic (while curling in a loop) and watch requests seamlessly shift. Bring it back and verify re-entry into rotation.

quiz.question: Making liveness probes depend on the database is dangerous because:
options: A DB blip marks every instance unhealthy at once — converting partial failure into total outage via restarts/removal
quiz.explanation: Liveness should answer 'is this process broken?'; shared-dependency failures belong in readiness, handled by not routing rather than mass-restarting.

items.title: Caching I: Layers and Cache-Aside
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Caching: The Cheapest Performance You'll Ever Buy

Every caching decision is the same trade: **staleness tolerance for speed**. The skill is knowing where and how stale.

## The layer map (request's journey)

1. **Browser** — Cache-Control headers; zero network
2. **CDN** — static assets & cacheable API responses at the edge (own lesson)
3. **API response cache** — output caching middleware
4. **Application cache** — IMemoryCache (per-instance) / Redis (shared)
5. **Database** — buffer pool (you know this one!), query result reuse

Cache as close to the user as staleness tolerance allows.

## Cache-aside (lazy loading) — the default pattern

```csharp
public async Task<Product?> GetProductAsync(int id)
{
    var key = $"product:{id}";
    var cached = await _redis.GetAsync<Product>(key);
    if (cached is not null) return cached;

    var product = await _db.Products.FindAsync(id);       // miss → source of truth
    if (product is not null)
        await _redis.SetAsync(key, product, TimeSpan.FromMinutes(10));  // TTL!
    return product;
}
```

Only requested data gets cached; cache failure degrades to DB (resilient). Costs: first-hit latency (miss penalty) and the eternal question — invalidation.

## IMemoryCache vs Redis

| | IMemoryCache | Redis |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | ~ns, no serialization | ~ms, network + serialization |
| Multi-instance coherence | ❌ each node its own view | ✅ one shared truth |
| Survives restart/deploy | ❌ | ✅ |
| Good for | reference data, per-node hot paths | sessions, shared state, invalidation-sensitive data |

Pro pattern: **two-level** — L1 IMemoryCache (30s) over L2 Redis (10min) over DB; L1 absorbs the hot-key hammering, short TTL bounds divergence. (.NET 9's HybridCache formalizes exactly this, plus stampede protection.)

Rule zero: **every cache entry gets a TTL**. Unbounded caches are memory leaks with good intentions.
items.practiceTask: Add cache-aside with IMemoryCache to your most-read endpoint (10-min TTL). Log hits/misses. Measure p50 latency before/after with a quick load test (bombardier/k6).

quiz.question: In cache-aside, the cache being down results in:
options: All requests fall through to the database — slower but functional (if the DB can take it)
quiz.explanation: Cache-aside treats the cache as optional acceleration; the DB remains the source of truth — though a cold DB under full load is its own risk (stampede lesson next).

items.title: Caching II: Invalidation, Stampedes, Hot Keys
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Hard Parts of Caching

"There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things." Here's the invalidation half.

## Invalidation strategies

| Strategy | How | Staleness | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TTL only** | let entries expire | up to TTL | trivial — start here |
| **Explicit eviction** | on write: `Remove(key)` | ~zero | every write path must know every affected key |
| Write-through | write cache + DB together | ~zero | write latency, orphaned entries on partial failure |
| Event-driven | writes publish events; subscribers evict | seconds | infra (pub/sub), eventual |

Pragmatic combo: **short-ish TTL as safety net + explicit eviction on the writes you control**. The TTL catches every path you forgot (there's always one — a batch job, another team's admin tool, manual SQL).

Versioned-key trick for hard-to-enumerate invalidations: key = `products:v{version}:list:{page}` — bump the version on any change; old entries die by TTL without enumeration.

## Cache stampede (dogpile)

Hot key expires → 500 concurrent requests all miss → 500 identical DB queries — a self-inflicted DDoS, potentially at the worst time (that's *why* the key was hot).

Defenses:
- **Per-key locking**: first miss computes; others wait for its result (`HybridCache` does this; manual: `SemaphoreSlim` per key)
- **Probabilistic early refresh**: refresh with increasing probability as expiry nears — one request pays, before the cliff
- **Serve-stale-while-revalidate**: return expired value, refresh in background

## Hot keys & big keys (Redis-specific)

One celebrity product hammered 50k/s concentrates load on one Redis shard: replicate read-only copies (`key:1..N`, random read) or absorb with L1 memory cache. Big keys (a 5MB list serialized) choke Redis's single-threaded core — keep values small, prefer hashes/fields over giant blobs.

## Metrics or it didn't happen

Track **hit ratio** per cache. <80% on a read-heavy path = wrong TTL, wrong keys, or wrong data cached. Cache size and eviction counts tell you when memory pressure silently disabled your cache.
items.practiceTask: Simulate a stampede locally: expire a hot key while 100 parallel tasks read through cache-aside; count DB hits. Add per-key SemaphoreSlim locking (or .NET 9 HybridCache) and show DB hits drop to 1.

quiz.question: A cache stampede is:
options: Many concurrent misses on the same expired hot key all hitting the backend simultaneously
quiz.explanation: The expiry cliff turns one hot key into N simultaneous backend queries; per-key locking or early refresh flattens it.

quiz.question: TTL + explicit eviction together (vs eviction alone) is recommended because:
options: TTL is the safety net for write paths you forgot or don't control — bounded staleness guaranteed
Eviction doesn't work on hot keys
quiz.explanation: Perfect eviction requires knowing every write path forever; TTL caps the damage of the one you missed.

items.title: CDNs and Static Content Strategy
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Content Delivery Networks

Geographically distributed edge servers caching your content near users. Cairo user ↔ your Frankfurt origin = ~60-100ms RTT; Cairo user ↔ Cairo edge = ~5-15ms. Physics is the one bottleneck you can't code away — CDNs move the content instead.

## What flows through a CDN

- **Static assets**: your Angular bundle, images, fonts, videos — the 90% case
- **Cacheable API responses**: public, identical-for-everyone GETs (product catalog, config) with proper Cache-Control
- Not (usually): personalized/authenticated responses

## Cache-busting: the versioned-filename contract

```
main.3f8a2b.js   ← hash in filename (Angular does this by default)
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable

index.html       ← references the hashed names
Cache-Control: no-cache   (revalidate every time)
```

Deploy = new hashes = new URLs; edges fetch new files naturally, no purge needed. `index.html` stays revalidated so users get the new references immediately. This filename-hash + immutable combo is why 'hard refresh fixes it' bugs mean someone broke the contract (e.g., cached index.html too long).

## Design impact on your architecture

- Origin traffic drops 80-95% for asset-heavy apps — your servers serve APIs, not bytes
- **Origin shield / request collapsing**: thousands of edge misses collapse to one origin fetch
- DDoS absorption: attacks hit the edge's capacity, not yours
- Angular deploys: `dist/` to Blob Storage + CDN (static hosting) — API and frontend scale independently; the ASP.NET server never sees an asset request

## Purge reality

Emergency invalidation (purge API) takes seconds-to-minutes to propagate across thousands of edges — good escape hatch, bad primary strategy. Versioned URLs make purging a non-event.
items.practiceTask: Inspect your Angular build output (ng build): confirm filename hashing. Then check response headers on a production site you use (DevTools → Network): identify CDN headers (x-cache: HIT/MISS, age) and the Cache-Control strategy for index.html vs hashed assets.

quiz.question: Hashed asset filenames + 'immutable, max-age=1 year' works safely because:
options: Any content change produces a new filename/URL — old cached entries are simply never referenced again
quiz.explanation: Content-addressed URLs make cache entries permanently valid: same URL = same bytes, forever.

items.title: Database Replication: Read Replicas and Failover
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Replication

Copies of your database on multiple servers, serving two distinct goals — don't conflate them:

1. **High availability**: primary dies → replica promoted → minutes-to-seconds of downtime instead of hours (SQL Server: Availability Groups)
2. **Read scaling**: route read traffic to replicas (readable secondaries), reserving primary capacity for writes

## Sync vs async — the fundamental dial

| Mode | Commit waits for replica? | Data loss on failover | Write latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | yes | zero (RPO=0) | + network round-trip per commit |
| **Asynchronous** | no | last few seconds possible | unaffected |

Typical prod: one sync replica (same region, failover target) + async replicas (read scaling / other regions, DR). You're re-meeting WRITELOG waits: sync replication adds to that same commit path.

## Replication lag — the bug factory

Async replicas run **seconds behind**. The classic:

> User saves profile (write→primary) → redirect → profile page reads replica → **their edit isn't there** → "the site lost my data!"

This is **read-your-own-writes** violation. Fixes:
- Route the writing user's reads to primary briefly (sticky-after-write: cookie/timestamp 'read primary for 10s after any write')
- Session consistency tokens (read waits until replica catches up to your write's LSN)
- Simply: read-after-write paths always hit primary; analytics/browsing hit replicas

## App architecture with replicas

```csharp
// Two contexts/connection strings; route by intent
services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(o => o.UseSqlServer(primaryCs));      // writes + RYW reads
services.AddDbContext<ReadDbContext>(o => o.UseSqlServer(replicaCs));    // queries (CQRS fits perfectly here)
```

Your CQRS lesson's query handlers get a natural home: query side → replicas, command side → primary. Connection string `ApplicationIntent=ReadOnly` routes to readable secondaries in an AG listener automatically.
items.practiceTask: Sketch your main app's endpoints in two columns: 'must read primary' (read-after-write paths) vs 'replica-safe' (lists, search, dashboards). What % of read traffic could offload? Note any endpoint where 5s of lag would cause a user-visible bug.

quiz.question: User saves data then immediately sees the old version on the next page. Classic cause in replicated setups:
options: The read went to an async replica still seconds behind the primary — read-your-own-writes violation
quiz.explanation: Async replication lag between the user's write and their next read; route post-write reads to primary or wait for replica catch-up.

items.title: Sharding: Splitting Data Across Databases
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning Across Servers)

When one database can't hold the write load or data volume, split rows across independent databases by a **shard key**. (Contrast: replication copies *all* data to each node; sharding gives each node a *slice*.)

## Choosing the shard key — the decision you can't cheaply undo

The ideal key: appears in ~every query, distributes load evenly, keeps related data together.

| Strategy | How | Pro | Con |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hash(key)** | `hash(TenantId) % N` | even distribution | range queries scatter; resharding moves everything |
| Range | key ranges per shard | range scans localized | hot ranges (newest dates = one hot shard!) |
| Directory | lookup table key→shard | flexible placement, per-tenant moves | the lookup service is critical infrastructure |

Multi-tenant SaaS: **TenantId** is the natural key — every query is tenant-scoped anyway, and one tenant's data stays co-located. (A tenant-per-database model is 'directory sharding' in practice.)

## What sharding breaks (pay attention)

- **Cross-shard queries**: 'top 100 across all tenants' = query every shard + merge in app (scatter-gather) — your k-way merge from DSA, now over HTTP
- **Cross-shard transactions**: no local ACID across shards → sagas/outbox (later lesson) or redesign so transactions stay shard-local
- **Cross-shard JOINs**: gone; denormalize or duplicate reference data to every shard
- **Unique IDs**: identity columns collide across shards → GUIDs (v7/sequential!), Snowflake-style IDs, or per-shard ranges
- **Resharding**: `% N` → `% N+1` remaps nearly every key. **Consistent hashing** (hash ring, virtual nodes) moves only ~1/N of data — the standard fix, and how Redis Cluster / Cassandra / DynamoDB partition

## The honest sequence

Shard **last**: after query optimization, caching, read replicas, archival, and vertical scaling are exhausted. A well-tuned SQL Server handles multiple TB and tens of thousands of transactions/sec — most systems never legitimately reach sharding. But *choose shard-friendly patterns early* (TenantId on every table, GUIDs where cross-DB uniqueness may matter): retrofitting a shard key into 400 queries is the real nightmare.
items.practiceTask: For your main work domain: pick the natural shard key, then audit — which existing queries lack it in the WHERE (would scatter-gather)? Which transactions would cross shards? Write the 5-line migration-pain assessment.

quiz.question: Consistent hashing beats hash(key) % N for sharding because:
options: Adding/removing a node remaps only ~1/N of keys instead of nearly all of them
quiz.explanation: Modulo arithmetic reassigns almost every key when N changes; the hash ring localizes the disruption to neighbors of the changed node.

quiz.question: For a multi-tenant SaaS, TenantId is usually the right shard key because:
options: Nearly every query filters by tenant, and a tenant's data stays co-located — minimal scatter-gather
quiz.explanation: The best shard key matches the natural access pattern; tenant-scoped queries route to exactly one shard.

items.title: CAP Theorem and Consistency Models
items.bodyMarkdown:
# CAP and What It Actually Means

In a distributed system, when a **network partition** happens (nodes can't reach each other — and it *will* happen), you must choose:

- **CP** — refuse requests you can't answer consistently (correct but partially unavailable)
- **AP** — keep answering from whatever data each side has (available but possibly stale/conflicting)

The common misreading: 'pick 2 of 3 at all times'. No — **P isn't optional** (networks fail); the C-vs-A choice only bites *during* partitions. Day-to-day, the real dial is **latency vs consistency** (PACELC: Else — even without partitions — you trade Latency for Consistency; sync replication = that trade).

## Where familiar systems sit

| System | Stance |
|---|---|
| SQL Server (single node) | CA territory — no partition possible inside one box |
| SQL Server AG sync replica | CP-leaning: commit waits for replica ack |
| Async read replicas | AP-leaning for reads (you met the lag) |
| Redis Cluster | mostly AP-ish; can lose acked writes in failover |
| Cosmos DB / DynamoDB | tunable per-request consistency |
| RabbitMQ mirrored queues | CP-leaning (prefers unavailability to divergence) |

## The consistency spectrum (know these four)

1. **Strong** — every read sees the latest committed write (single-node SQL; distributed = expensive coordination)
2. **Read-your-own-writes** — *you* see your writes; others may lag (the replication lesson's fix)
3. **Monotonic reads** — you never see data go *backwards* in time (session stickiness)
4. **Eventual** — replicas converge, eventually; any read may be stale (DNS, CDNs, most caches)

## The business translation skill

Per *data item*, ask: what does 5 seconds of staleness cost?
- Account balance shown before transfer: annoying → acceptable
- Double-spending that balance: money → strong consistency on the *write path* (the atomic-guard UPDATE!)
- Product view count: nobody cares → eventual, aggressively cached

Systems mix models: strong where money moves, eventual where eyeballs browse. Declaring one consistency level for a whole system is the junior take; per-operation choice is the senior one.
items.practiceTask: For 5 data items in your domain (e.g., stock quantity, order status, profile photo, permissions, audit log): assign the weakest acceptable consistency model and justify in one sentence each. Note which ones your current caching already quietly downgraded.

quiz.question: During a network partition, a CP system responds by:
options: Serving possibly-stale data to stay available
Refusing/erroring requests it cannot answer consistently
quiz.explanation: CP sacrifices availability: better no answer than a wrong one. AP makes the opposite call.

items.title: Message Queues: Decoupling with Async Work
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Message Queues

Producer writes a message; consumer processes it **later, independently**. The single most useful architectural upgrade after caching.

## What a queue buys you

- **Latency**: user's request returns in 50ms; the email/PDF/report happens async
- **Load leveling**: 10k spike arrives → queue absorbs → consumers drain at their sustainable rate (vs 10k concurrent threads melting your DB)
- **Failure isolation**: email provider down ≠ checkout down; messages wait
- **Independent scaling**: 20 lightweight API pods, 3 beefy worker pods

## The mental shift: at-least-once + idempotency

Queues typically guarantee **at-least-once** delivery: a consumer that crashes after processing but before acknowledging gets the message **again**. Exactly-once delivery is a myth at the transport level — instead:

> **Every consumer must be idempotent** — processing the same message twice = same outcome as once.

Techniques: natural idempotency (`SET Status='Shipped'` is safely repeatable), dedup table (processed MessageIds, checked in the same transaction as the work), or upserts keyed by message id. This rule is so central it gets its own lesson.

## Poison messages & DLQs

A message that always crashes its consumer would retry forever, blocking everything. After N failures → **dead-letter queue** for humans to inspect. Always configure the DLQ, always monitor its depth (a growing DLQ is a silent outage).

## .NET landscape

| Tool | Sweet spot |
|---|---|
| Azure Service Bus | full-featured cloud queues/topics, sessions, DLQs |
| RabbitMQ | self-hosted standard, flexible routing |
| **MassTransit / NServiceBus** | abstraction + retries + sagas + outbox over both — start here |
| Hangfire | job-queue-in-SQL-Server — zero new infra, perfect first step |
| Channel<T> | in-process producer/consumer (lost on crash — different animal) |

```csharp
// The shape of the win (MassTransit)
await publishEndpoint.Publish(new OrderPlaced(order.Id));   // fire and return
// elsewhere, scaled independently:
public class SendConfirmationConsumer : IConsumer<OrderPlaced> { ... }
```

Queue depth is your health gauge: rising = consumers can't keep up (scale them) or are failing (check DLQ).
items.practiceTask: Take one slow synchronous side-effect in your app (email, report, image resize). Move it behind a queue (Hangfire is the lowest-friction start on SQL Server). Measure endpoint latency before/after, then kill the worker mid-job and verify the retry.

quiz.question: Consumers must be idempotent because:
options: At-least-once delivery redelivers messages after consumer crashes/timeouts — duplicates are a matter of when, not if
quiz.explanation: Ack-after-processing means a crash between processing and ack yields redelivery; idempotency makes the duplicate harmless.

quiz.question: A dead-letter queue exists to:
options: Quarantine messages that repeatedly fail so they stop blocking the queue, preserving them for diagnosis
quiz.explanation: Poison messages would retry forever; the DLQ removes them from the hot path without losing the evidence.

items.title: Pub/Sub and Event-Driven Architecture
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Events: Inverting the Dependency Arrows

**Queue (command)**: 'do this' — one intended handler (`SendWelcomeEmail`).
**Event (pub/sub)**: 'this happened' — `OrderPlaced` broadcast; zero to N subscribers each get a copy, publisher neither knows nor cares.

That reversal is your Observer pattern at system scale — and the OCP payoff is identical: **new reactions without touching the publisher**. Marketing wants a Slack ping on big orders? New subscriber. Checkout service unchanged, unredeployed.

## Topology (Service Bus terms; RabbitMQ ≈ exchanges+bindings)

```
OrderPlaced → topic ─┬→ subscription: email-service      (its own queue)
                     ├→ subscription: inventory-service
                     └→ subscription: analytics  (filter: Total > 1000)
```

Each subscription = independent queue: separate consumers, retries, DLQs. One slow subscriber doesn't delay others.

## Designing events well

- **Past tense, business language**: `OrderPlaced`, `PaymentFailed` — facts, not instructions
- **Fat vs thin**: thin (id only — subscribers call back for data: always fresh, but N callbacks and coupling to your API) vs fat (full payload: self-contained, but versioning matters and data is as-of-publish). Default: carry what most subscribers need; version deliberately (`OrderPlacedV2`)
- **Events are contracts**: renaming a field breaks unknown consumers — additive changes only, or new versions

## The traps (equal time)

- **Invisible flow**: 'what happens when an order is placed?' is now answered by *searching subscriptions across repos*. Invest in correlation ids + tracing (observability lesson) from day one
- **Eventual consistency**: subscribers lag — the UI can't assume inventory reflects the order it just placed
- **Ordering**: most brokers guarantee order only per partition/session key (`OrderId` as key ⇒ per-order ordering). Global ordering: give it up
- **Choreography vs orchestration**: chains of 'A's event triggers B, B's triggers C' become invisible workflows nobody can follow. Multi-step *processes* deserve an explicit orchestrator (saga lesson)

And the deepest one — publishing reliably alongside your DB transaction — is the outbox lesson's whole subject.
items.practiceTask: Identify one 'when X happens, also do Y and Z' flow currently inline in your code. Sketch it as event + subscribers: name the event (past tense), decide fat/thin payload, choose the partition key for ordering, and list which subscriber failures matter.

quiz.question: The OCP-style payoff of pub/sub over direct service calls is:
options: New subscribers add behavior with zero changes to the publisher — reactions are open for extension
quiz.explanation: The publisher emits facts; who reacts is someone else's configuration. Debugging actually gets harder — that's the trade.

items.title: Idempotency: Surviving Retries Everywhere
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Idempotency

An operation is **idempotent** if doing it twice has the same effect as once. In distributed systems, retries are everywhere — HTTP clients, queue redelivery, users double-clicking, Polly policies you added yourself — so **duplicates are guaranteed input**, and idempotency is the immune system.

## The money scenario

```
Client → POST /payments (charge $100)
     → network timeout... did it charge? ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
     → retry POST /payments
     → charged twice? 💸💸
```

The timeout is indistinguishable from 'server processed it and the response was lost'. Retrying is correct client behavior — the server must dedupe.

## Idempotency keys — the standard

```csharp
// Client generates a unique key per logical operation (not per attempt!)
POST /payments
Idempotency-Key: 7f3d2c9a-...

// Server:
public async Task<IResult> Charge(PaymentRequest req, [FromHeader(Name="Idempotency-Key")] string key)
{
    var existing = await db.IdempotencyRecords.FindAsync(key);
    if (existing is not null)
        return Results.Ok(existing.StoredResponse);        // replay saved result — no double charge

    var result = await ProcessPayment(req);
    db.IdempotencyRecords.Add(new(key, Serialize(result))); // same transaction as the work!
    await db.SaveChangesAsync();
    return Results.Ok(result);
}
```

Critical details: the dedup record commits **in the same transaction** as the business change (else crash-between = duplicate anyway); keys expire after ~24-72h; concurrent same-key requests need a unique constraint (second insert fails → fetch and replay).

Stripe, PSPs, and every serious payment API work exactly this way — recognize the header.

## Cheaper idempotency where available

| Technique | Example |
|---|---|
| Naturally idempotent ops | `PUT /users/5 {state}` (replace), `SET Status='Shipped'`, DELETE |
| Guarded transitions | `UPDATE ... WHERE Status='Pending'` — @@ROWCOUNT 0 = already done (your SQL atomic guard!) |
| Unique constraints | dedup table on MessageId/OrderNumber — the DB rejects the duplicate |
| Upserts | MERGE keyed by external id |

HTTP semantics recap: GET/PUT/DELETE idempotent *by contract*; POST is not — hence the key header for POSTs that create/charge/send.
items.practiceTask: Add idempotency-key handling to one 'dangerous if repeated' endpoint (order creation, message send). Prove it: two identical POSTs → one DB effect, identical responses. Bonus: make two *concurrent* same-key requests behave via the unique constraint.

quiz.question: A client that times out on POST /payments should retry with the same idempotency key because:
options: It's faster than a new key
Timeout can't distinguish 'never processed' from 'processed, response lost' — the key lets the server dedupe either way
quiz.explanation: Same logical operation = same key across attempts; the server either executes once and replays the stored response, or executes fresh.

quiz.question: The idempotency record must be written in the same DB transaction as the business change because:
options: A crash between the change and the record would leave the operation replayable — duplicating it despite the mechanism
quiz.explanation: Atomicity of work + dedup-marker is the guarantee; split them and the crash window reintroduces duplicates.

items.title: The Outbox Pattern: Reliable Events with Your Database
items.practiceTask: Implement a minimal outbox in your LearnMore-style stack: OutboxMessages table, transactional write alongside a business change, BackgroundService relay that logs 'published'. Kill the relay mid-batch and verify no message is lost and duplicates are possible (then explain why that's fine).

quiz.question: The outbox pattern solves dual-write by:
options: Using two-phase commit with the broker
Persisting the event in the same DB transaction as the business change; a relay publishes it afterwards
quiz.explanation: Atomicity exists within one database — the outbox exploits it, converting 'publish' into data that survives crashes.

items.title: Distributed Transactions and Sagas
items.bodyMarkdown:
# When One Transaction Can't Cover It

Order placement touching Orders DB + Payment service + Inventory service: no shared transaction exists. **Two-phase commit (2PC)** technically exists (DTC) but couples availability (all participants must be up, locks held across the network) — effectively dead in cloud/microservice practice.

## Sagas: sequence of local transactions + compensations

Each step is a **local, committed transaction**. If a later step fails, run **compensating transactions** to undo the earlier ones — business-level undo, not rollback:

```
ReserveInventory ✅ → ChargePayment ✅ → CreateShipment ❌
                                            ↓ compensate backwards
            RefundPayment ← ReleaseInventory
```

Compensation ≠ rollback: the charge *happened* (statement shows charge+refund). Some steps can't un-happen (email sent) — order steps so hard-to-compensate ones come **last**, and prefer *reservations* over irreversible actions early (hold inventory, don't decrement; authorize payment, capture late).

## Orchestration vs choreography

| | Choreography | Orchestration |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | each service reacts to events, emits next | central saga/state machine directs each step |
| Flow visibility | scattered across services | one place to read the whole process |
| Coupling | low, but implicit | services know the orchestrator |
| Fits | 2-3 steps, simple | 3+ steps, branches, timeouts, human approvals |

Rule of thumb: two steps → choreograph; a real *process* → orchestrate (MassTransit state machine sagas persist state in your SQL Server, handle timeouts — 'payment pending > 30min → cancel' — and survive restarts).

## Design checklist for any saga

1. Every step **idempotent** (retries!) — you have this tool
2. Every step's **compensation defined** before you build it (if you can't compensate it, move it last)
3. Timeouts as first-class transitions (waiting-for-payment isn't forever)
4. The saga's own state persisted transactionally (outbox again)
5. A human-visible dashboard of stuck sagas — they *will* get stuck; 'stuck at step 3 for 2 days' must be findable

And the escape worth naming: sometimes the right answer is **don't distribute** — if Orders and Inventory always change together, maybe they're one service with one database and one honest ACID transaction. Saga complexity is a tax; make sure the service split earns it.
items.practiceTask: Design (on paper) the saga for your domain's most multi-system flow: steps, compensations, step ordering by reversibility, timeout policy per step. Mark any step with no possible compensation and propose the reservation-style redesign.

quiz.question: In a saga, a failed step is handled by:
options: Rolling back all databases via DTC
Executing compensating transactions that business-level-undo the already-committed earlier steps
Retrying the whole saga from step 1
quiz.explanation: Earlier steps committed locally and can't roll back; compensation (refund, release, cancel) reverses their business effect.

quiz.question: Hard-to-compensate steps (sending an email, capturing payment) should be:
options: Ordered last, after reversible/reservable steps have all succeeded
quiz.explanation: Sequencing by reversibility minimizes the window where an irreversible action needs undoing.

items.title: Rate Limiting and Backpressure
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Protecting Your System from Its Callers

Without limits, one aggressive client (or a bug in a loop, or a scraper) consumes capacity meant for everyone. Rate limiting is fairness + self-defense.

## The algorithms

| Algorithm | Behavior | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed window | N per minute, reset at :00 | boundary burst: 2N straddling the reset |
| Sliding window | N per rolling 60s | smooth, slightly more state |
| **Token bucket** | tokens refill at rate r, burst up to bucket size b | the practical favorite: steady rate + tolerated bursts |
| Concurrency limit | max N *in flight* | protects slow endpoints regardless of rate |

## ASP.NET Core has it built in (.NET 7+)

```csharp
builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(o =>
{
    o.AddPolicy("per-user", ctx => RateLimitPartition.GetTokenBucketLimiter(
        partitionKey: ctx.User.Identity?.Name ?? ctx.Connection.RemoteIpAddress!.ToString(),
        _ => new TokenBucketRateLimiterOptions {
            TokenLimit = 20, ReplenishmentPeriod = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1),
            TokensPerPeriod = 5, QueueLimit = 0 }));
    o.RejectionStatusCode = 429;
});
app.UseRateLimiter();
```

Partition by **user/API key** (fair) > by IP (corporate NAT = thousands share one IP; mobile carriers too). Return **429 + Retry-After** — well-behaved clients (and your own Polly policies) respect it.

Multi-instance note: in-process limiters count per instance (N instances ≈ N× the intended limit) — usually acceptable; strict global limits need a shared store (Redis token bucket) or the gateway layer.

## Backpressure: the deeper principle

Rate limiting rejects at the door; **backpressure** propagates 'slow down' through the whole pipeline: bounded queues (`Channel.CreateBounded` blocks/rejects producers when full), `429`/`503 + Retry-After` telling upstream to back off, consumer lag pausing producers. The alternative to backpressure is unbounded buffering — which is just choosing OOM as your failure mode.

Load shedding, the last resort: under extreme pressure, deliberately drop *low-value* work (analytics beacons) to preserve *high-value* work (checkout). Deciding priorities **before** the incident is the actual engineering.
items.practiceTask: Add the built-in rate limiter to your API with a token bucket per user. Hammer it with a parallel loop; verify 429s + Retry-After, then implement a client that honors Retry-After and confirm graceful behavior.

quiz.question: Token bucket is generally preferred over fixed windows because:
options: It allows controlled bursts while enforcing a steady average rate, without window-boundary spikes
quiz.explanation: Refill rate sets the average; bucket capacity absorbs legitimate bursts; no reset-boundary double-spend.

quiz.question: An unbounded in-memory queue between a fast producer and slow consumer eventually causes:
options: Out-of-memory failure — unbounded buffering is deferred collapse; bounded queues + backpressure are the fix
quiz.explanation: If inflow exceeds outflow with no bound and no signal upstream, memory is the only thing absorbing the difference — until it isn't.

items.title: API Design: Contracts, Versioning, Pagination
items.bodyMarkdown:
# APIs Are Contracts

Once someone you don't control calls your API, every response shape, status code, and field name is a promise. Breaking promises breaks *their* production.

## REST resource discipline (the 20% that matters)

- Nouns for resources, HTTP verbs for actions: `GET /orders/42`, `POST /orders`, not `POST /getOrder`
- Status codes carry semantics: 200/201/204, 400 (your request is wrong) vs 422 (understood but invalid), 401 (who are you) vs 403 (you can't), 404, 409 (conflict — optimistic concurrency!), 429 (limits lesson)
- **Error bodies standardized**: ASP.NET Core's `ProblemDetails` (RFC 7807) — machine-readable type/title/detail, one shape for every error
- Async ops: `202 Accepted + Location: /operations/123` for long-running work — pairs with your queue lesson

## Breaking vs non-breaking (memorize the line)

| Safe (additive) | Breaking |
|---|---|
| New optional field in response | Removing/renaming a field |
| New endpoint | Changing a field's type/format |
| New optional query param | New required param |
| | Changing status codes/semantics |

Corollary for clients: **tolerant reader** — ignore unknown fields (System.Text.Json default), never fail on additions.

## Versioning: everyone's favorite bikeshed

URL (`/api/v2/orders`) — visible, cacheable, ugly; header/media-type — purist, invisible; query param — pragmatic. **Choosing one and staying consistent matters 10× more than which.** Version when you *must* break, support N-1 for a real deprecation window (with `Sunset` headers and comms), and design additively to version rarely. `Asp.Versioning` handles the plumbing.

## Pagination, filtering, field selection

Cursor/keyset over offset for anything large (your SQL lesson — same reasoning at the API layer: `?after=cursor` beats `?page=5000`), and the cursor is opaque to clients (base64 of your keyset — you can change its internals). Cap page sizes (`?limit=100` max). Consistent filter grammar (`?status=open&createdAfter=...`).

One more contract: **ids are opaque strings** to clients. Exposing int identity invites enumeration attacks (`/orders/1,2,3...`) and welds your DB key strategy to the public contract — GUIDs/v7 or at minimum non-guessable ids on anything sensitive.
items.practiceTask: Audit one of your production APIs against this lesson: standardized ProblemDetails? 400-vs-422/401-vs-403 correct? Any offset pagination on big tables? Any enumerable int ids on sensitive resources? Fix or ticket the top two findings.

quiz.question: Which change is safe to ship without versioning?
options: Adding a new optional field to the response
Changing date format from ISO to Unix epoch
quiz.explanation: Additive changes don't break tolerant readers; renames, type changes, and new requirements break existing callers.

items.title: Monolith vs Microservices: The Real Trade
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Architecture Decision Everyone Gets Backwards

Microservices don't primarily solve *technical* scale — a load-balanced monolith scales horizontally just fine (you built one in lesson 2). They solve **organizational** scale: 8 teams can't share one codebase, one release train, one 2 AM deploy freeze without stepping on each other.

## What microservices actually cost

Every in-process call that becomes a network call inherits: latency (+network round trip), partial failure (your resilience lesson), serialization, versioned contracts (API lesson), distributed transactions → sagas (that lesson), 'where did my request go' → distributed tracing (next lessons). Plus per-service CI/CD, infra, on-call. **You've now studied ~6 lessons of machinery that exists only because of the network boundary.** That's the price tag, itemized.

## The modular monolith: the underrated default

```
MyApp.sln
├─ Modules/Ordering/     ← own DbContext (own schema), public interface, internal everything else
├─ Modules/Inventory/    ← talks to Ordering ONLY via its public interface or in-proc events
├─ Modules/Identity/
└─ Host/                 ← one deployable wiring modules together
```

Module boundaries enforced at compile time (`internal`, architecture tests like NetArchTest), separate schemas, module-scoped transactions, events between modules (MediatR notifications). You get: monolith deployment simplicity + microservice-shaped seams. **If a module later truly needs independence** (team ownership, divergent scaling, different tech), it extracts cleanly — the interface becomes an API, the events go on a bus, the schema moves out.

## When extraction is actually justified

- A team needs to deploy independently of others (organizational)
- A component's scaling profile is wildly different (10k RPS image resizing vs 50 RPS admin)
- Divergent runtime needs (Python ML service in a .NET shop)
- Genuine fault isolation requirement (payment must survive catalog's death)

'It's more scalable' and 'Netflix does it' are not on the list. Start modular-monolith; extract when a *specific* boundary earns its network tax; expect most never to.

Distributed monolith — services that must deploy together, share a database, chain synchronous calls — is both prices, neither benefit: the worst quadrant. If services can't fail independently, they aren't independent services.
items.practiceTask: Map your current work system's modules and their coupling (shared tables? synchronous call chains? shared deploys?). Identify: one boundary that would extract cleanly, and one 'distributed monolith' symptom if you already have services.

quiz.question: The strongest legitimate driver for extracting a microservice is:
options: Independent team ownership/deployment or a genuinely divergent scaling/runtime profile
quiz.explanation: The network boundary's costs are justified by organizational independence or hard technical divergence — not by fashion or size alone.

quiz.question: A 'distributed monolith' is a system where:
options: A monolith runs on many servers
Services are physically separate but must deploy together / share data / can't fail independently — paying network costs without gaining independence
One service is much larger than others
quiz.explanation: Coupled services inherit all distribution costs (latency, partial failure, tracing) while retaining monolith coordination costs.

items.title: API Gateways and BFF
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Front Door

With multiple services (or even a modular monolith exposing multiple APIs), clients shouldn't juggle N base URLs, N auth schemes, N retry policies. An **API gateway** is the single entry point that handles the cross-cutting edge concerns once:

- **Routing**: `/api/orders/*` → ordering service; path rewriting
- **AuthN at the edge**: validate JWT once; forward identity claims to services (which still authorize!)
- Rate limiting, request size limits, IP filtering (your limits lesson, applied globally)
- TLS termination, response compression/caching
- Telemetry: one place where every request is counted and timed

.NET-native option: **YARP** (reverse proxy as an ASP.NET Core app — config-driven routes, full middleware power). Cloud: Azure API Management / Front Door.

```json
// YARP essence
"Routes": { "orders": { "ClusterId": "ordering", "Match": { "Path": "/api/orders/{**rest}" } } },
"Clusters": { "ordering": { "Destinations": { "d1": { "Address": "http://ordering:8080" } } } }
```

Gateway anti-pattern: business logic creeping into it (the gateway becomes a bottleneck monolith owned by nobody). Routing, auth, limits — yes; order validation — no.

## BFF: Backend-for-Frontend

Different clients want different shapes: your Angular app needs a dashboard = 1 call, not 6 service calls from the browser (latency × 6, CORS × 6, token juggling). A **BFF** is a thin API tailored to one frontend:

```
Angular ↔ BFF (aggregates 6 calls server-side, shapes DTOs for screens, holds tokens in HttpOnly cookies)
Mobile  ↔ its own BFF (smaller payloads, different aggregation)
```

Security bonus for SPAs: the **BFF holds the OAuth tokens server-side** (browser gets only an HttpOnly session cookie) — the current OWASP-recommended SPA auth pattern, eliminating tokens-in-localStorage XSS theft.

Gateway vs BFF: gateway = generic edge infrastructure (one for everyone); BFF = per-client aggregation layer owned by the frontend team (one per client type). They stack: Client → BFF → gateway → services.
items.practiceTask: Stand up YARP in front of your API(s): one route, JWT validation at the proxy, rate limiting middleware. Then sketch your Angular app's chattiest screen — how many calls would a BFF endpoint collapse into one?

quiz.question: The BFF pattern's security benefit for SPAs is:
options: OAuth tokens live server-side in the BFF; the browser holds only an HttpOnly cookie — XSS can't exfiltrate tokens
It removes the need for auth
quiz.explanation: Tokens never reach JavaScript-accessible storage; the BFF exchanges the cookie-bound session for tokens when calling downstream.

items.title: Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Observability: Debugging Systems You Can't Attach To

Monitoring tells you *that* something's wrong; observability lets you ask *why* without shipping new code. Three pillars, three different questions:

## 1. Structured logs — "what happened here?"

```csharp
// ❌ string soup — unqueryable
log.LogInformation($"Order {id} failed for {email}");
// ✅ structured — every property queryable
log.LogInformation("Order {OrderId} failed for {CustomerEmail} with {Reason}", id, email, reason);
```

Message templates → Seq/ELK/App Insights can answer 'all failures for this customer, this week'. Rules: log levels mean something (Warning = investigate weekly, Error = investigate today); **correlation id on every line** (see traces); never log secrets/PII; logs are expensive at volume — Info for business events, Debug off in prod.

## 2. Metrics — "is the system healthy?"

Cheap aggregated numbers over time: request rate, error rate, latency percentiles, queue depth, cache hit ratio, DLQ depth (your earlier lessons keep resurfacing). The RED method per service: **R**ate, **E**rrors, **D**uration.

**Percentiles, not averages**: average latency 80ms can hide p99 = 4s — and 1% of your traffic is your unhappiest users, often your biggest customers (more data = slower queries). Alert on p95/p99 and error *rate* (not count).

## 3. Distributed traces — "where did this request go?"

One request → gateway → API → SQL + Redis + queue → consumer. A trace stitches it into a waterfall: every hop a **span** (with timing + metadata), all sharing a **trace id** propagated via the `traceparent` header (W3C standard).

.NET makes this almost free: **OpenTelemetry** auto-instruments ASP.NET Core, HttpClient, SQL, and `ILogger` correlation:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenTelemetry()
    .WithTracing(t => t.AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
                       .AddHttpClientInstrumentation()
                       .AddSqlClientInstrumentation()
                       .AddOtlpExporter())      // → Jaeger/Tempo/App Insights
    .WithMetrics(m => m.AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation().AddOtlpExporter());
```

The payoff moment: 'checkout is slow' → open trace → 40 sequential SQL spans (N+1! — you know that lesson) or one 3s external-API span. Minutes, not days.

Event-driven flows: propagate trace context in message headers, or the queue hop breaks the story (MassTransit does this automatically). Your invisible-flow problem from the pub/sub lesson? This is its answer.
items.practiceTask: Add OpenTelemetry (tracing + metrics) to your app with console or Jaeger exporter (docker run jaegertracing/all-in-one). Hit a page and read its trace: find the SQL spans, and deliberately create an N+1 to see it visually.

quiz.question: Alerting on average latency instead of p99 is dangerous because:
options: Averages hide tail pain — 1% of requests can be catastrophically slow (often for your heaviest users) while the average looks fine
quiz.explanation: Latency distributions are skewed; the tail is where timeouts, retries, and unhappy big customers live.

quiz.question: Distributed tracing connects spans across services via:
options: A trace id propagated in headers (W3C traceparent) through every HTTP call and message
quiz.explanation: Context propagation is the whole trick — each hop continues the same trace id, letting the backend assemble the waterfall.

items.title: Deployments: Zero Downtime and Rollback
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Shipping Without Stopping

'Deploy at 2 AM Sunday' is an architecture smell. Zero-downtime deployment is a solved problem — with a database asterisk.

## Strategies

| Strategy | How | Trade |
|---|---|---|
| **Rolling** | replace instances one-by-one behind the LB | both versions live simultaneously (compat burden) |
| **Blue/green** | full new environment, switch traffic atomically | 2× infra during deploy; instant rollback = switch back |
| **Canary** | new version gets 1%→5%→50%→100% of traffic, watching metrics | needs traffic splitting + automated rollback triggers |
| Feature flags | deploy dark, enable per user/tenant/percentage | **decouples deploy from release** — the real superpower |

Common thread: **two versions run at once** during every non-atomic strategy. Your code must tolerate it.

## The database is the hard part

App v2 wants `CustomerName` split into First/Last. Deploy v2 + migration together → v1 instances (still running mid-rollout!) crash on the changed schema. The fix: **expand/contract** — every schema change is backward-compatible for one deploy cycle:

```
EXPAND:   add nullable FirstName/LastName; v2 writes BOTH old and new columns, reads new-else-old
MIGRATE:  backfill job populates new columns from old
CONTRACT: (next release) v3 stops touching CustomerName; later, drop it
```

Rules that follow: never rename (add + migrate + drop), never drop in the same release that stops using it, migrations run *before* code deploys and must work with the *previous* app version. EF Core migrations fit — but review the generated SQL for lock behavior on big tables (`ALTER TABLE` can block; your blocking lesson applies).

## Rollback honesty

Rollback = deploying the old version — which only works if the DB (expand/contract!) and *emitted events/messages* remain compatible. Data written by v2 in a new format that v1 can't read = you can't actually roll back. **Roll-forward** (fix fast) is often the honest strategy; feature flags give you instant 'rollback' of behavior without redeploying at all.

Health-check integration: rolling deploys wait for the new instance's **readiness** probe before shifting traffic and killing the next old instance — your health check lesson is literally the deploy gate.
items.practiceTask: Take your last schema change and rewrite it as expand/contract phases. Then simulate a rolling deploy locally: run v1 and v2 of your API simultaneously against the expanded schema and verify both work.

quiz.question: Renaming a column in the same release that deploys the code using the new name breaks rolling deploys because:
options: Old-version instances still running during the rollout hit a schema they don't understand
Renames require downtime by SQL design
The LB can't route during migrations
quiz.explanation: Non-atomic rollouts mean old and new code share one schema for minutes-to-hours; expand/contract keeps both compatible.

quiz.question: Feature flags' core deployment benefit is:
options: Decoupling deploy from release — code ships dark and activates instantly per cohort, with instant behavioral rollback
quiz.explanation: The riskiest moment stops being the deploy; enabling (and disabling) behavior becomes a runtime decision.

items.title: Real-Time: WebSockets, SignalR, and Alternatives
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Pushing Data to Clients

HTTP is request/response; 'the server tells the browser something changed' needs other machinery.

## The options ladder

| Technique | How | Fit |
|---|---|---|
| Short polling | client asks every Ns | dead simple; latency = interval; wasteful at scale |
| Long polling | server holds the request until data or timeout | near-real-time without WS; connection churn |
| **SSE** (Server-Sent Events) | one HTTP stream, server→client only | feeds, notifications, LLM token streams — underrated |
| **WebSockets** | full-duplex persistent TCP | chat, collab editing, games — the full solution |

## SignalR: WebSockets for grown-ups (.NET)

Handles negotiation (WS → SSE → long-poll fallback), reconnection, multiplexing, and RPC-style messaging:

```csharp
public class NotificationHub : Hub
{
    public override async Task OnConnectedAsync() =>
        await Groups.AddToGroupAsync(Context.ConnectionId, $"user:{Context.UserIdentifier}");
}

// From anywhere server-side (e.g., your queue consumer!):
await hubContext.Clients.Group($"user:{userId}").SendAsync("orderShipped", orderId);
```

```typescript
// Angular
this.connection = new signalR.HubConnectionBuilder()
    .withUrl('/hubs/notifications').withAutomaticReconnect().build();
this.connection.on('orderShipped', id => this.store.markShipped(id));
```

## Where real-time meets your other lessons

- **Statelessness breaks**: a WebSocket lives on ONE server. Server A can't push to a socket on server B → **backplane** (Redis pub/sub: `AddSignalR().AddStackExchangeRedis(...)`) broadcasts across instances — or offload entirely to **Azure SignalR Service**
- **Scale math changes**: 20k idle REST users ≈ no load; 20k WebSockets = 20k open connections *held* — memory per connection, LB connection limits, and deploys now *disconnect everyone* (auto-reconnect + resume matters)
- **Missed messages**: reconnecting clients missed pushes — real-time is a *notification* layer, not the source of truth. Pattern: push 'something changed' → client fetches authoritative state via REST (or track sequence numbers)

Decision shortcut: dashboards/notifications → SSE or SignalR-over-SSE; chat/presence/collab → SignalR WebSockets + Redis backplane; 'refresh every 30s is honestly fine' → polling, don't overbuild.
items.practiceTask: Add a SignalR hub to your app pushing a server event to a toast in Angular (e.g., 'daily item completed' celebration). Then run two API instances and observe cross-instance push failing without a backplane; add Redis backplane and see it fixed.

quiz.question: A Redis backplane (or Azure SignalR) becomes necessary with multiple instances because:
options: Each socket is pinned to one server; pushing to a user connected elsewhere needs cross-instance message distribution
quiz.explanation: Hub broadcasts must reach connections held by other instances; the backplane republishes every send to all servers.

quiz.question: Clients that reconnect after a dropped WebSocket should:
options: Re-fetch authoritative state via the API — pushes are notifications, not the source of truth
Wait for the server to replay automatically
quiz.explanation: Messages pushed during the gap are gone (absent explicit replay design); reconcile from the durable store.

items.title: Full-Text Search and Search Architecture
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Search Is Not WHERE LIKE

`WHERE Name LIKE '%laptop%'` — leading wildcard = full scan (your SARGability lesson), no relevance ranking, no typo tolerance, no 'gaming laptops' understanding. Search is a different data structure problem.

## The inverted index (the core idea)

Instead of rows → words, map **words → documents**:

```
"laptop"  → [doc3, doc7, doc19]
"gaming"  → [doc7, doc42]
query "gaming laptop" → intersect → [doc7], ranked by relevance (TF-IDF/BM25:
rare-in-corpus terms matching often in a short doc score highest)
```

Plus analysis at index time: lowercase, tokenize, **stemming** (running→run), synonyms, language rules. That's how 'Running Shoes' matches 'run shoe'.

## Your options, in escalation order

**1. SQL Server Full-Text Search** — built in, zero new infra:
```sql
CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX ON Products(Name, Description) KEY INDEX PK_Products;
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE CONTAINS((Name, Description), 'NEAR((gaming, laptop), 5)');
-- or ranked: FREETEXTTABLE / CONTAINSTABLE with RANK
```
Handles stemming, thesaurus, ranking. Weaker at: typo tolerance, faceting, complex relevance tuning. **Right answer for most business apps** — exhaust it before adding infrastructure.

**2. Elasticsearch / OpenSearch / Azure AI Search** — dedicated engines: fuzzy matching, facets/aggregations, per-field boosting, highlighting, vectors. Cost: a second data store you must **sync** (outbox → indexer! your lesson again) — search results can lag writes (eventual consistency, again), reindexing strategy, cluster ops.

**3. Vector/semantic search** — embeddings + k-NN: 'affordable computer for games' matches without shared words. Hybrid (keyword + vector) is the modern default for product/document search; SQL Server 2025 and all the engines above are adding native vector types.

## The architecture seam

Search results are **pointers, not truth**: index stores what's needed to find + display a result list; clicking through loads the authoritative record from SQL. That keeps the index lean and staleness harmless (a result row with a slightly-old price corrects on the detail page).
items.practiceTask: Enable SQL Full-Text on a text-heavy table in LocalDB (install the feature if needed). Compare LIKE '%term%' vs CONTAINS for: plan/reads, stemmed matches ('running' finds 'run'), and ranked output via CONTAINSTABLE.

quiz.question: An inverted index makes text search fast because:
options: It precomputes word→documents mappings, turning a query into list intersection instead of scanning every row's text
quiz.explanation: Like your prefix-sum lesson: pay at write time to make reads near-instant — the search-shaped version of that trade.

quiz.question: Adding Elasticsearch alongside SQL Server introduces which recurring problem from this course?
options: Dual-write/sync — keeping the index consistent with the source of truth (outbox/indexer, eventual consistency)
quiz.explanation: A second store always raises 'how does data get there reliably and how stale can it be' — the same outbox + eventual-consistency machinery.

items.title: File Storage: Object Stores, Uploads, and Signed URLs
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Files Don't Belong on Your Web Server

Local disk fails the 2-instance test (uploaded to A, requested from B), dies with the instance, and doesn't scale. Databases *can* hold blobs but buy bloated backups, buffer-pool pollution (8KB pages full of JPEG!), and expensive storage. The standard answer: **object storage** (Azure Blob / S3).

## Object storage mental model

Not a filesystem: a flat key→blob map (`tenant42/invoices/2026/inv-991.pdf` is a *key*, the folders are illusion). Practically infinite, ~11 nines durability (replicated), tiered pricing (hot/cool/archive), lifecycle rules (auto-move old files to cheap tiers, auto-delete temp).

Database stores **metadata**: `Documents(Id, TenantId, BlobKey, FileName, Size, ContentType, UploadedBy, ...)` — queryable, transactional, small. Blob stores bytes.

## The upload pattern that scales: don't proxy bytes

❌ Browser → your API (buffers 200MB) → blob storage: your instances become expensive byte pumps (memory, sockets, request limits).

✅ **Direct upload with a SAS (shared access signature) / presigned URL**:

```
1. Angular → POST /api/documents/upload-url { fileName, size }     (authZ happens HERE)
2. API → validates, creates metadata row (status: Pending),
         returns short-lived write-only SAS URL for a specific blob key
3. Angular → PUT bytes directly to blob storage (progress bar, retries — SDK handles)
4. Angular → POST /api/documents/{id}/complete   (or Event Grid notification)
   → API verifies blob exists, marks metadata Active
```

Your API handles a few JSON round-trips; storage handles the bytes at its own scale. Downloads mirror it: short-lived read SAS per authorized request (never public container access for user data; the URL expiry *is* the authorization window).

## Safety checklist

- Validate content-type by **magic bytes** server-side (extension lies; an .exe renamed .pdf is still an exe)
- Never serve user uploads from your app domain without `Content-Disposition: attachment` + correct content-type (stored XSS via uploaded HTML/SVG)
- Malware scan async (queue lesson) for user-shared files; images: re-encode (strips exploits + EXIF GPS)
- Blob keys: GUIDs, not user filenames (traversal/collision); orphan cleanup job for Pending-forever rows
items.practiceTask: Implement the SAS-URL upload flow against Azurite (local Azure Storage emulator): endpoint issuing a write SAS, Angular direct PUT, completion callback verifying blob existence. Confirm your API never touches the file bytes.

quiz.question: Presigned/SAS direct uploads beat proxying files through your API because:
options: Your instances stop pumping bytes — storage absorbs the bandwidth/memory load while your API handles only small authZ round-trips
quiz.explanation: AuthZ still happens (issuing the scoped, short-lived URL is the auth decision); the heavy I/O just bypasses your compute tier.

items.title: Back-of-Envelope Estimation
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Numbers Every Architect Should Feel

System design decisions are quantitative. Estimation isn't precision — it's catching **order-of-magnitude** errors before building ('will this fit in one SQL Server?' is answerable on a napkin).

## Anchor numbers (memorize the magnitudes)

| Operation | Cost |
|---|---|
| Memory read | ~100 ns |
| SSD random read | ~100 μs (1000× memory) |
| Same-DC network round trip | ~0.5-1 ms |
| Cross-region round trip | ~50-150 ms |
| SQL point query (warm) | ~0.5-2 ms |
| Redis GET | ~0.2-0.5 ms + network |

| Scale fact | Value |
|---|---|
| 1 day | ~86,400 s (~10⁵) |
| 1M requests/day | **~12 RPS** average (!), ~30-50 peak |
| One modern server | 10k+ simple RPS |
| SQL Server, tuned | thousands of TPS, TBs of data |

That '1M/day ≈ 12 RPS' regularly deflates over-engineering on the spot.

## The method (worked example: your company's e-invoicing)

**Assume**: 50k businesses × 40 invoices/day = 2M invoices/day.

1. **Write load**: 2M / 86,400 ≈ **23 writes/s** (peak ×5 ≈ 115/s) → one SQL Server yawns
2. **Storage**: invoice row ~2KB × 2M/day ≈ **4GB/day** ≈ 1.5TB/year → fine; PDFs 100KB × 2M = **200GB/day** → object storage + lifecycle tiers, NOT the database (files lesson)
3. **Reads**: 10 reads per invoice write = 230/s → cache + maybe a replica; easy
4. **Bandwidth**: PDF downloads 0.5M/day × 100KB ≈ 50GB/day ≈ **5 Mbps average** → trivial; CDN if spiky
5. **Conclusion in one sentence**: 'SQL Server + blob storage + one cache handles this for years; the only scale problem is PDF storage volume — lifecycle policy from day one.'

That sentence — deliverable of the estimate — is what changes the design meeting.

## Interview/meeting protocol

State assumptions out loud (they'll be corrected — that's good data). Round aggressively (86,400→100k). Compute per-second, storage per-year, bandwidth average and peak. Compare each number to an anchor ('23 wps vs thousands SQL handles'). Land on the *binding constraint* — the one number that actually shapes the architecture.
items.practiceTask: Estimate your current work system: requests/s (from real daily traffic), storage growth/year, and peak bandwidth. Identify the binding constraint. Then check your estimates against production metrics — calibrate where you were >10× off.

quiz.question: A service expecting 5 million requests/day needs, on average, infrastructure for roughly:
options: ~60 RPS (with peaks maybe 3-5×) — a single well-built instance's territory
quiz.explanation: 5M / 86,400s ≈ 58 RPS. The day-to-seconds division routinely reveals 'big' numbers to be small.

items.title: Authentication at Scale: JWT, Sessions, OAuth
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Identity Across Services

## Session vs token — the fundamental trade

| | Server session | JWT |
|---|---|---|
| State | server-side (Redis at scale) | in the token — server stateless |
| Validation | lookup per request | signature check, no I/O |
| **Revocation** | instant (delete session) | **hard — valid until expiry** |
| Cross-service | services share session store | any service validates with the public key |

JWTs win at service-to-service and API scale; their Achilles heel is revocation. The standard resolution: **short-lived access tokens (5-15 min) + refresh tokens**:

```
access token (15 min, stateless validation everywhere)
refresh token (days, stored server-side, REVOCABLE, rotated on each use)
→ logout/compromise = revoke refresh; damage window = access token's remaining minutes
→ refresh rotation + reuse detection: a replayed old refresh token reveals theft → kill the family
```

## JWT hygiene (the audit checklist)

- Validate **issuer, audience, expiry, signature** — ASP.NET Core's `AddJwtBearer` does this; don't disable pieces
- Asymmetric signing (RS256): auth server holds the private key; services fetch public keys via JWKS and validate offline
- Claims: ids and roles, not PII (JWTs are base64, **readable by anyone** — signed ≠ encrypted)
- SPA storage: not localStorage (XSS-readable) → HttpOnly cookies or the BFF pattern (your gateway lesson)
- Clock skew exists: 1-2 min tolerance, don't panic at 'expired 10 seconds ago' logs

## OAuth2/OIDC in one paragraph

OAuth2 = *delegated authorization* framework; **OIDC** = identity layer on top (id_token, /userinfo). Your cases: SPA/mobile → **Authorization Code + PKCE** (never implicit flow, never password grant); service-to-service → **Client Credentials**. Don't build the auth server: Microsoft Entra ID, Auth0, Keycloak, or Duende IdentityServer — password storage, MFA, token lifecycles and their CVEs are someone else's full-time job.

## AuthZ at scale (one rule)

Gateway authenticates; **every service still authorizes** ("does user 42 own order 991?" — resource-level checks in the service that owns the resource). Trusting 'the gateway checked' for object-level access = one SSRF/misroute from IDOR.
items.practiceTask: Decode (jwt.io) a real access token from any system you use — inventory its claims for PII. Then audit your API's AddJwtBearer config: issuer/audience validation on? Access token lifetime? Refresh rotation? Write the two most urgent fixes.

quiz.question: Short-lived access tokens + revocable refresh tokens exist because:
options: Stateless JWTs can't be revoked — shrinking their lifetime bounds the damage window while refresh tokens provide the revocation point
Refresh tokens are faster to validate
quiz.explanation: The pattern reconciles stateless validation (scale) with the ability to cut off a compromised session (security).

quiz.question: Putting a user's email and full name inside a JWT is risky because:
options: It makes the token expire faster
JWT payloads are merely base64-encoded — readable by anything that sees the token; signed is not encrypted
quiz.explanation: Anyone holding the token (browser extensions, logs, proxies) reads the payload; keep claims to opaque ids and roles.

items.title: Case Study: Design a URL Shortener
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Design bit.ly — The Classic Warm-Up

Deceptively simple; every lesson shows up. Walk the method.

## 1. Requirements & estimation

Functional: shorten(long)→short; redirect(short)→301/302; optional custom aliases, expiry, click stats.
Scale assumption: 100M new URLs/month ≈ **40 writes/s**; 100:1 read:write ≈ **4k redirects/s**. Storage: ~500B/URL × 100M/mo ≈ 50GB/month — trivial. **Binding constraint: redirect read path latency+volume.**

## 2. The short code

Base62 (a-zA-Z0-9): 62⁷ ≈ **3.5 trillion** codes at 7 chars — enough forever.

| Generation | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Hash the URL (MD5, take 7) | collisions need probing; same URL = same code (feature? leak?) |
| Random + uniqueness check | retry loop under volume |
| **Counter → base62** | collision-free, trivial — but sequential = enumerable (competitor scrapes your traffic) |
| Counter × modular multiplicative inverse (or pre-generated shuffled key pool) | collision-free AND non-guessable ✅ |

Distributed counters without coordination: allocate **ranges** per instance (server A: 1M-2M) — your sharding lesson's ID problem, solved the same way.

## 3. Read path (where the design lives)

```
GET /abc123 → LB → API → Redis GET code:abc123 (hit: ~0.3ms, 90%+)
                        ↓ miss
                    SQL point lookup by PK (2ms) → warm cache → 301
```

Cache-aside, TTL + LFU eviction; hot links (viral tweet) = hot key lesson → L1 in-process cache absorbs. 4k RPS of sub-ms lookups: **2-3 stateless API instances + one Redis + one SQL Server**. Resist the urge to shard — the napkin says no.

## 4. Redirect status code — the sneaky trade

**301 (permanent)**: browsers cache → next click skips you entirely → fast for users, **but you lose click analytics**. **302/307**: every click hits you → stats, control, expiry works properly. Business answer: 302 when analytics is the product. (Interviewers love this detail.)

## 5. Click stats without killing the read path

Don't `UPDATE Clicks SET Count=Count+1` inline (write per redirect + hot row lock — your locking lesson). **Fire-and-forget event → queue → aggregate in batches** (or Redis INCR flushed periodically). Redirect latency stays pure; stats are eventual — nobody needs real-time-exact click counts.

## 6. Expiry & abuse

Expiry: check on read (lazy) + background sweep (your batching lesson). Abuse: rate-limit creation (token bucket per key), malware-domain blocklist on create + async re-scan (queues again).

**The pattern to internalize**: estimate → find the binding constraint → design the hot path around it → push everything non-critical off the hot path (async) → deliberately refuse unneeded scale machinery.
items.practiceTask: Build the core in your stack: counter-range allocation, base62 encode, Redis cache-aside redirect, queued click counting (Channel<T> is fine locally). Load test the redirect path (bombardier) and report p99 with/without cache.

quiz.question: Choosing 302 over 301 for redirects is a business decision because:
options: 301 lets browsers cache the redirect — subsequent clicks bypass your service, killing analytics and expiry control
quiz.explanation: Permanent-redirect caching trades your visibility/control for client-side speed; if click data is the product, 302 wins.

quiz.question: Incrementing a click counter synchronously in the redirect path is avoided because:
options: It adds a write + hot-row contention to the latency-critical path for data that's fine being eventual — queue and batch it
quiz.explanation: Hot path purity: anything not required to serve the redirect moves to async aggregation.

items.title: Case Study: Design a Chat System
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Design WhatsApp-Lite

Real-time + persistence + fan-out: the lessons compose.

## 1. Requirements & the hard parts

1:1 + group chat, delivery/read receipts, online presence, history, offline delivery. Scale: 1M DAU × 40 msgs ≈ 40M msgs/day ≈ **460/s average, ~2-3k/s peak**. Message ~200B → 8GB/day → partition-by-time territory within a year (SQL partitioning lesson: archival by SWITCH).

Hard parts: **connection management** (millions of long-lived sockets), **fan-out** (group of 200 = 200 deliveries per message), **offline sync**, **ordering**.

## 2. Connections

WebSockets (SignalR), sticky-by-necessity: each user's socket lives on one node. A **connection registry** (Redis: `user:42 → node-7`) or a Redis backplane routes messages to the right node. 1M concurrent sockets ÷ ~50k per node ≈ 20 gateway nodes — connection tier scales independently of logic tier (your microservice-extraction criteria: genuinely different scaling profile!).

## 3. The message path (every lesson at once)

```
sender → gateway node → API:
  1. persist message (SQL: PK (ConversationId, MessageId↑))   ← durability FIRST
  2. ack sender ('sent ✓')
  3. queue fan-out job                                          ← off the hot path
consumer: for each recipient → registry lookup →
  online: push via their node ('delivered ✓✓' when client acks)
  offline: leave in their sync backlog (+ mobile push notification)
```

Persist-then-fanout = crash-safe (outbox thinking). Delivery is **at-least-once** → client dedupes by MessageId (idempotency lesson, client edition).

## 4. Ordering & ids

Global ordering: impossible-ish and unnecessary. **Per-conversation ordering** suffices (pub/sub lesson's partition-key insight — ConversationId is the key). MessageId: **Snowflake-style** (timestamp bits + node bits + sequence) — time-sortable, globally unique, no coordination; doubles as the ordering key and the sync cursor.

## 5. Offline sync = keyset pagination

Client reconnects with its last seen MessageId per conversation → `WHERE ConversationId=@c AND MessageId > @cursor ORDER BY MessageId` — your keyset lesson, verbatim, on the clustered key. No missed messages (they're in SQL, not in the ether — 'real-time is notification, DB is truth').

## 6. Presence — the deceptive feature

Naive 'broadcast every online/offline flip' = N² storm (500 contacts × every app backgrounding). Reality: heartbeat every 30s → TTL'd Redis key (`presence:42`, EXPIRE 60) → miss = offline; **pull presence on chat-open + subscribe only to open conversations**' participants. Accept 30-60s staleness (consistency-spectrum lesson: presence is the definition of eventual-is-fine).

Read receipts at group scale get the same treatment: batched, eventual, aggregated ('read by 47').
items.practiceTask: Extend your SignalR experiment into mini-chat: persist-then-push with per-conversation ordering (identity or timestamp ids), a sync endpoint using keyset pagination from a client cursor, and TTL-based Redis presence. Kill the connection mid-conversation, reconnect, verify zero message loss.

quiz.question: Messages are persisted to the database BEFORE fan-out delivery because:
options: Durability first: a crash mid-fan-out loses pushes but not messages — offline sync from the DB recovers everything
Push notifications need a row id
quiz.explanation: The DB is the source of truth; delivery is a retryable notification layer on top. Reverse the order and crashes eat messages.

quiz.question: Presence uses heartbeat + TTL'd keys instead of connect/disconnect events because:
options: Disconnects are unreliable (crashes, network drops never fire 'clean' disconnect) — expiry-based liveness self-heals
quiz.explanation: Absence of heartbeat is the only trustworthy offline signal; TTL turns it into an automatic mechanism.

items.title: Case Study: Design a News Feed
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Design the Feed — Fan-out Economics

The canonical read-vs-write trade-off study.

## 1. The two strategies

**Pull (fan-out on read)**: feed request = query posts of all N followees, merge by time, return top 50.
- Write: O(1) — insert post, done
- Read: N queries + k-way merge (your heap lesson!) *per feed view* — and users view feeds ~50× more than they post 💀
