**Push (fan-out on write)**: on post, insert a feed entry into **every follower's** precomputed feed list.
- Read: O(1) — `LRANGE feed:42 0 49` from Redis, single-digit ms ✅
- Write: O(followers) — 5M followers = 5M inserts *per celebrity post* 💀

## 2. The answer is always hybrid

| User posts | Strategy |
|---|---|
| Normal users (99.9%, <10k followers) | **push** — async fan-out via queue (spikes absorbed, your load-leveling lesson) |
| Celebrities/hot accounts | **pull** — followers' feed reads merge celebrity posts in at read time |

Feed read = precomputed list (push part) + live merge of followed-celebrities' recent posts (pull part). Two sorted sources, one merge — DSA again.

## 3. Storage sketch

Posts: SQL, source of truth. Feeds: **Redis lists/sorted-sets per user, capped ~800 entries** — a feed is a *cache of pointers* (post ids), rebuildable from the social graph if evicted; store ids not payloads (post edits/deletes stay consistent — search lesson's 'pointers not truth' principle). Hydrate posts by id (multi-get, cached) at render.

Estimation: 10M DAU × 800 × 16B ≈ 128GB Redis — real but affordable; capping and inactive-user eviction is why you cap.

## 4. Ranking

Chronological = simple, transparent. Ranked (engagement/ML) changes architecture: candidate generation (recent from feed) → scoring service (features: recency, affinity, engagement velocity) → re-rank top ~500. Score at read time (fresh, costly) or pre-score at fan-out (stale, cheap) — same push/pull trade one level up.

## 5. The remaining machinery (all reruns)

- New-post distribution: **outbox** → fan-out workers (reliability lesson)
- Fan-out worker crash mid-celebrity? Idempotent inserts (sorted-set add = naturally idempotent ✅)
- Feed pagination: **cursor by post id/score**, not offset (posts arrive between pages — offset double-shows)
- Unfollow/block: lazy filter at read (remove-from-feed is O(feed) — filter cheaper)
- Counters (likes): batched/eventual, never synchronous hot-row increments (URL-shortener lesson)

**The interview meta-skill**: you didn't invent anything new today — you *composed* 12 prior lessons. That's what system design actually is.
items.practiceTask: Implement mini-feed locally: follow graph in SQL, push fan-out via a queue worker into Redis sorted sets (score = timestamp), hybrid read merging one 'celebrity' user's posts at read time, cursor pagination. Measure feed-read latency vs the pure-pull SQL version at 100k posts.

quiz.question: Push (fan-out on write) is preferred for normal users because:
options: Feed reads vastly outnumber posts — precomputing at write time moves cost to the rare operation and makes the frequent one O(1)
quiz.explanation: Optimize for the dominant operation: 50 reads per write means write-time work amortizes brilliantly — until follower counts explode it.

quiz.question: Celebrity accounts switch to pull because:
options: Fan-out on write = one insert per follower; at millions of followers a single post becomes millions of writes — merging at read time caps the cost
quiz.explanation: The hybrid inverts strategy exactly where write amplification crosses over read amplification.

items.title: Designing for Failure: Timeouts, Bulkheads, Chaos
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Everything Fails — Design for It

Distributed systems' first law: dependencies **will** fail — slowly, partially, and at the worst time. Resilience is designed, not patched. (You have retry/circuit-breaker from the patterns track; this is the architecture layer around them.)

## Slow is worse than down

A *dead* dependency fails fast; a *slow* one holds your threads, sockets, and memory hostage while requests pile up — then **you** die, and whoever calls you follows. Cascading failure is latency propagation. Hence:

- **Timeouts on everything** — every HttpClient, SQL command, Redis call. Budget them: if your SLA is 2s, downstream budget is maybe 500ms each, not 30s defaults
- **Circuit breakers** convert slow into fast-fail (patterns track)
- **Bulkheads**: partition resources so one dependency can't drain the pool — separate HttpClient pools per dependency, bounded concurrency per integration (`SemaphoreSlim`/Polly bulkhead), even separate thread pools. Ship-compartment logic: flood one, ship floats

## Degrade gracefully — the product conversation

For each dependency, decide *before the incident* what its failure means:

| Dependency down | Degraded behavior (not error page!) |
|---|---|
| Recommendations service | show bestsellers (static fallback) |
| Search | category browse still works |
| Payment | accept order, queue capture, email confirmation later |
| Analytics | drop silently (load-shedding lesson) |

Fallbacks are product decisions wearing engineering clothes — get them agreed with the business, then encode (Polly fallback policies).

## Verify it actually works: chaos engineering

Resilience code that's never exercised is Schrödinger's resilience. Netflix's insight: **inject failures on purpose** — kill instances, add latency, blackhole a dependency — in controlled experiments (staging first, prod eventually, blast-radius-limited). Even manual game days ('unplug Redis, watch dashboards, follow the runbook') find the gaps: the retry storm you misconfigured, the health check that takes the whole fleet down (LB lesson!), the fallback that NPEs.

## Blast radius thinking

Cell-based architecture: partition users across independent stacks (cells) — an incident hits one cell = 10% of users, not all. Regional isolation, per-tenant pools for whale customers — same idea at different granularity. The question for every design review: **'when this fails, who is affected, and how do we make that set smaller?'**
items.practiceTask: Run a mini game day on your app: (1) add 5s artificial latency to your DB or an external call (toxiproxy or a middleware delay), (2) observe threads/latency/timeouts cascade, (3) add timeout + circuit breaker + one graceful fallback, (4) re-run and document the difference.

quiz.question: A slow dependency is more dangerous than a dead one because:
options: It silently consumes your threads/connections while requests queue — resource exhaustion cascades upstream before anyone alarms
Retries don't work on slow services
quiz.explanation: Fast failure releases resources immediately; slowness holds them at scale — timeouts + breakers exist to convert slow into fast-fail.

quiz.question: Bulkheads in service design mean:
options: Partitioned resource pools (connections, concurrency) per dependency so one failing integration can't drain everything
quiz.explanation: Like ship compartments: isolation limits the flood — the recommendation service's outage keeps its own pool, not checkout's.

items.title: The System Design Interview Method
items.bodyMarkdown:
# A Repeatable Method (Interview & Real Life)

The case studies followed a hidden script. Here it is, explicit — 45 minutes structured:

## 1. Requirements (5 min) — *talk, don't design*

- **Functional**: core flows only, cut scope out loud ('v1: no groups, no editing — agreed?')
- **Non-functional**: scale (DAU? ratio reads:writes?), latency targets, consistency needs per feature (your CAP lesson's per-item skill), availability target
- Interview signal: juniors design features; seniors interrogate constraints first

## 2. Estimation (5 min)

Napkin math → **binding constraint** (estimation lesson). Say the conclusion: 'writes are trivial, reads need a cache tier, storage forces lifecycle policy'. The estimate *drives* the design; skipping to boxes-and-arrows without it is decoration.

## 3. API + data model (5-10 min)

Core endpoints (REST discipline), key entities, the ids (Snowflake? GUID? — you know the trade-offs), the one or two critical indexes/access patterns. This grounds everything after.

## 4. High-level design (10 min)

Draw the request path: client → CDN/LB/gateway → services → cache/DB/queue. **Start boring**: LB + stateless API + SQL + Redis + one queue covers a shocking fraction of systems. Every box must earn its place with a sentence tied to a requirement.

## 5. Deep-dive the binding constraint (10-15 min)

The interviewer picks, or you propose: the hot path (shortener's redirect), the fan-out (feed), the connection tier (chat). Go lesson-deep: exact caching strategy + invalidation, exact consistency trade + failure mode + degraded behavior. One area deep > everything shallow.

## 6. Failure & evolution sweep (5 min)

Walk your diagram: 'this dies → what happens?' (bulkheads/fallbacks/lesson-of-failure). Then: 'at 10× scale, what breaks first?' — name the next bottleneck and its known fix. This sweep is where senior signal concentrates.

## The trade-off reflex (the actual skill being tested)

Every statement pairs with its cost: 'Cache here — accepting up to 60s staleness, which is fine for X because Y.' 'Push fan-out — paying write amplification to buy O(1) reads, hybrid for celebrities.' Naming what you *give up* is what separates architecture from feature-listing. If you can't say what a choice costs, you haven't made a choice — you've followed a fashion.

And in real life: the method is the same, minus the clock — plus writing it down (ADRs: context → decision → consequences) so the next engineer knows *why*.
items.practiceTask: Run the full method solo, 45 minutes timed, on 'design a ride-sharing dispatch system' (or notification service). Produce: requirements list, napkin math + binding constraint, API sketch, diagram, one deep-dive, failure sweep. Review yourself against the trade-off reflex — count how many decisions name their cost.

quiz.question: The estimation step matters in system design because:
options: It identifies the binding constraint that should drive the architecture — without it, box-drawing is unjustified decoration
quiz.explanation: Design follows numbers: 12 RPS and 12k RPS deserve different systems, and only the estimate tells you which one you're building.

quiz.question: The strongest senior signal in design discussions is:
options: Stating each decision's trade-off — what is gained, what is paid, and why it's acceptable here
quiz.explanation: Architecture is choosing among costs; articulating the cost you accepted proves the choice was deliberate.
